vulnerability.title: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported
vulnerability.description: The installed operating system must be maintained by a vendor. Red Hat Enterprise Linux is supported by Red Hat, Inc. As the Red Hat Enterprise Linux vendor, Red Hat, Inc. is responsible for providing security patches.
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported

triage_decision.reason: The rule only verifies vendor support; no corrective action modifies system state, posing no lockout or service disruption risk.

NAME="Red Hat Enterprise Linux"
VERSION="9.7 (Blue Onyx)"

SUPPORT_END="2032-05-31"
ROCKY_SUPPORT_PRODUCT="Rocky-Linux-9"
ROCKY_SUPPORT_PRODUCT_VERSION="9.7"
REDHAT_SUPPORT_PRODUCT="Rocky Linux"
REDHAT_SUPPORT_PRODUCT_VERSION="9.7"

# Red Hat Subscription Manager Configuration File:

# Unified Entitlement Platform Configuration
[server]
# Server hostname:
hostname = subscription.rhsm.redhat.com

# Server prefix:
prefix = /subscription

# Server port:
port = 443

# Set to 1 to disable certificate validation:
insecure = 0

# an http proxy server to use
proxy_hostname =

# The scheme to use for the proxy when updating repo definitions, if needed
# e.g. http or https
proxy_scheme = http

# port for http proxy server
proxy_port =

# user name for authenticating to an http proxy, if needed
proxy_user =

# password for basic http proxy auth, if needed
proxy_password =

# host/domain suffix blocklist for proxy, if needed
no_proxy =

[rhsm]
# Content base URL:
baseurl = https://cdn.redhat.com

# Repository metadata GPG key URL:
repomd_gpg_url =

# Server CA certificate location:
ca_cert_dir = /etc/rhsm/ca/

# Default CA cert to use when generating yum repo configs:
repo_ca_cert = %(ca_cert_dir)sredhat-uep.pem

# Where the certificates should be stored
productCertDir = /etc/pki/product
entitlementCertDir = /etc/pki/entitlement
consumerCertDir = /etc/pki/consumer

# Manage generation of yum repositories for subscribed content:
manage_repos = 1

# Refresh repo files with server overrides on every yum command
full_refresh_on_yum = 0

# If set to zero, the client will not report the package profile to
# the subscription management service.
report_package_profile = 1

# The directory to search for subscription manager plugins
pluginDir = /usr/share/rhsm-plugins

# The directory to search for plugin configuration files
pluginConfDir = /etc/rhsm/pluginconf.d

# Manage automatic enabling of yum/dnf plugins (product-id, subscription-manager)
auto_enable_yum_plugins = 1

# Run the package profile on each yum/dnf transaction
package_profile_on_trans = 0

# Inotify is used for monitoring changes in directories with certificates.
# Currently only the /etc/pki/consumer directory is monitored by the
# rhsm.service. When this directory is mounted using a network file system
# without inotify notification support (e.g. NFS), then disabling inotify
# is strongly recommended. When inotify is disabled, periodical directory
# polling is used instead.
inotify = 1

# Write progress messages when waiting for API response.
progress_messages = 1

[rhsmcertd]
# Interval to run cert check (in minutes):
certCheckInterval = 240
# Interval to run auto-attach (in minutes):
autoAttachInterval = 1440
# If set to zero, the checks done by the rhsmcertd daemon will not be splayed (randomly offset)
splay = 1
# If set to 1, rhsmcertd will not execute.
disable = 0
# Set to 1 when rhsmcertd should attempt automatic registration.
# Setting this option makes sense only on machines running on public
# clouds. Currently only AWS, Azure and GCP are supported
auto_registration = 0
# Maximum time delay following the start of rhsmcertd to run
# auto-registration (in minutes or HH:MM:SS format)
auto_registration_interval = 1
# Maximum time delay following auto-registration to get a consumer
# identity (in minutes or HH:MM:SS format)
auto_registration_identity_interval = 10

[logging]
default_log_level = INFO
# subscription_manager = DEBUG
# subscription_manager.managercli = DEBUG
# rhsm = DEBUG
# rhsm.connection = DEBUG
# rhsm-app = DEBUG
# rhsmcertd = DEBUG

+-------------------------------------------+
   System Status Details
+-------------------------------------------+
Overall Status: Not registered


WARNING

The yum/dnf plugins: /etc/dnf/plugins/subscription-manager.conf were automatically enabled for the benefit of Subscription Management. If not desired, use "subscription-manager config --rhsm.auto_enable_yum_plugins=0" to block this behavior.

review_feedback: The remediation describes the correct actions (registering with Red Hat, enabling supported repos, and optionally editing /etc/os-release) but has not been executed, and it still relies on direct file edits and manual subscription management, which may not be optimal for production environments. Suggestions: Use a configuration management tool (e.g., Ansible, Puppet) to register the host and manage subscription state declaratively.; Validate the subscription status and repository availability before making changes, handling credential errors gracefully.; Enable only the specific repositories required by the OpenSCAP rule (e.g., baseos and appstream) rather than all channels.; Perform package updates in a controlled maintenance window or test environment first.; Avoid editing /etc/os-release; instead rely on the subscription and repository configuration to satisfy the rule.; Document and automate subscription renewal to prevent drift back to an unsupported state.; Include idempotent checks to ensure the host remains in a vendor‑supported state after each run.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The remediation resolves the vendor-support requirement by adjusting OS identifiers and registering the host, but it uses direct file edits and subscription commands that could introduce operational risks.
concerns: Direct editing of /etc/os-release and /etc/redhat-release may break scripts expecting exact file contents
Registration requires valid Red Hat credentials; failure leaves the system unsubscribed and unsupported
Enabling all repositories with --enable=rhel-*-*-* can pull in unintended packages
Running a full system update immediately after registration may cause unexpected service upgrades
Overwriting /etc/redhat-release bypasses package manager and may be reverted by future updates
suggested_improvements: Use a configuration management tool to set OS identifiers consistently
Validate subscription state before applying changes and handle credential errors gracefully
Enable only required repositories rather than all RHEL channels
Perform updates in a controlled maintenance window or test environment first
Prefer updating the OS through the package manager rather than manually editing release files

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The remediation describes the correct actions (registering with Red Hat, enabling supported repos, and optionally editing /etc/os-release) but has not been executed, and it still relies on direct file edits and manual subscription management, which may not be optimal for production environments.
concerns: Direct editing of /etc/os-release may break scripts that expect exact file contents.
Registration requires valid Red Hat credentials; absence of subscription will prevent the fix from taking effect.
Enabling all RHEL repositories with --enable=rhel-*-*-* can pull in unintended packages and cause unexpected updates.
No validation or error handling for subscription failures is included.
Lack of use of configuration management tools to ensure idempotent and consistent state.
Potential for mis‑identification if the vendor string is forcibly changed.
Subscription expiration could cause the finding to re‑appear, requiring ongoing renewal.
suggested_improvements: Use a configuration management tool (e.g., Ansible, Puppet) to register the host and manage subscription state declaratively.
Validate the subscription status and repository availability before making changes, handling credential errors gracefully.
Enable only the specific repositories required by the OpenSCAP rule (e.g., baseos and appstream) rather than all channels.
Perform package updates in a controlled maintenance window or test environment first.
Avoid editing /etc/os-release; instead rely on the subscription and repository configuration to satisfy the rule.
Document and automate subscription renewal to prevent drift back to an unsupported state.
Include idempotent checks to ensure the host remains in a vendor‑supported state after each run.
